main_sections.title: What a Tank!
main_sections.prose_summary: The chapter is an informational text about water storage structures, focusing on traditional and modern water tanks in India. It opens by noting that water conservation has existed for centuries, with ancient structures serving multiple purposes — storing water, preventing floods, and uniting communities. The text describes traditional methods still in use: Ooranis in Tamil Nadu for rainwater harvesting, Panam Keni wells in Kerala that stay full year-round, and underground Tankas in Rajasthan's Thar region that store water for six to seven months. It then shifts to modern overhead tanks, common in most Indian homes, before arriving at its centrepiece: the uniquely designed overhead water tanks of rural Punjab. Unlike plain tanks elsewhere, Punjab's rural tanks are sculpted from cement and steel into shapes like flowers, footballs, roosters, tractors, aeroplanes, and statues — each design reflecting the owner's identity, occupation, or passion. The text explains the construction process (steel frame, concrete finish, rooftop installation) and celebrates these tanks as cultural expressions and community landmarks. The tone is informative and celebratory, inviting learners to appreciate both the utility and artistry of water storage.

question_bank.stem: What do we use a water tank for?
Where is Punjab located on the map of India?
How are the water tanks in Punjab made?
How do the unique shapes of water tanks reflect the identity of the house owners?

oracy.section_name: Let us Think + Let us Speak + Let us Listen

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
B. Think and answer.
1. An overhead water tank is a way to store water. Do you know any other ways of storing water?
2. If you had to build a water tank, what would you make it look like? Why?

question_bank.stem: An overhead water tank is a way to store water. Do you know any other ways of storing water?
If you had to build a water tank, what would you make it look like? Why?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Sit in groups of three and discuss:
• How do we waste water in our daily lives?
• What can we do to stop this wastage?
For example: Using running water to brush teeth wastes a lot of water. We can use a tumbler or a mug to save water.
B. Write at least three such examples (of water wastage and ways to stop it) and share with the class.

C. Say aloud, 'What a tank!' and 'Water tank'.
• Do both phrases use the word 'tank'? Yes/No
• Do they mean the same thing? Yes/No
Now, say aloud, 'I scream' and 'Ice cream'.
• Do both phrases sound the same? Yes/No
• Do they mean the same thing? Yes/No

Let us Listen: Listen to your teacher read out the following passage and choose the correct option.

A Day without Water
One hot summer morning, Suma woke up and went to brush her teeth. But when she turned on the tap, no water came out! She was surprised. Her mother said, 'There's no water in the tank. The whole area has no water today.' Suma quickly realised how difficult it was to live without water. She couldn't brush her teeth, wash her face, or take a bath. In the kitchen, there was no water to cook food or clean the dishes. At school, the toilets were closed, very little drinking water was available, and the children were told to bring water bottles from home. Suma saw that people in her neighbourhood were upset. Some ran to the nearby shop to buy water bottles, while others waited near a water tanker that came from another place. Everyone looked worried. That evening, the water was finally restored. Suma felt very happy and promised herself that she would never waste water again. She decided to tell her friends how precious water is. Suma's school also announced a Water Conservation Week. They planned activities like poster-making, planting trees, and even a water-saving competition. Suma and her friends made posters with slogans like 'Save Every Drop' and 'No Water, No Life.' After this experience, Suma understood how important water tanks and water-saving habits are. She asked her parents to set up a rainwater harvesting system at home. Her parents agreed happily.

question_bank.stem: Why was Suma surprised in the morning?
options: There was no water in the tap.

question_bank.stem: How did people manage without water?
options: They bought bottles and waited for tankers.

question_bank.stem: What did Suma promise after the water supply was restored?
options: To check the water tank frequently.

question_bank.stem: What activities were planned during the Water Conservation Week?
What did Suma suggest to her parents?
options: To fix a rainwater harvesting system.

tasks_verbatim.transcript_text: A Day without Water: One hot summer morning, Suma woke up to find no water from the tap. Her mother explained the whole area had no water. Suma couldn't brush teeth, wash, or bathe. At school, toilets were closed, drinking water was scarce, and children were told to bring water bottles. Neighbours ran to buy bottles or waited for tankers. That evening, water was restored. Suma promised never to waste water. Her school announced a Water Conservation Week with poster-making and tree-planting. Suma made posters: 'Save Every Drop' and 'No Water, No Life.' She later asked her parents to set up rainwater harvesting at home, and they agreed.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Short forms (or contractions) are when two words are combined and some letters are left out. An apostrophe (') takes the place of the missing letters. For example: aren't = are not; wouldn't = would not.

A. Write the contractions for the following words.
1. do not
2. is not
3. are not
4. cannot
5. could not
6. did not

question_bank.stem: Write the contraction for: do not
Write the contraction for: is not
Write the contraction for: are not
Write the contraction for: cannot
Write the contraction for: could not
Write the contraction for: did not

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
B. Rewrite the following underlined words using contracted forms.
1. Partho is not feeling well today. His parents have decided that he will stay at home till he gets better. Partho is bored and cannot stay at home anymore. He decides to ask his grandmother if he can go outside, but he thinks she, too, would not agree.
2. It is a rainy day, and Tina is not happy. She cannot go out to play with her friends. Her brother does not want to play indoors either. They have decided to watch a movie instead. Their mother says she will make popcorn for them.

question_bank.stem: Rewrite paragraph 1 replacing underlined words with contractions: 'Partho is not feeling well...'
Rewrite paragraph 2 replacing underlined words with contractions: 'It is a rainy day...'

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
C. Read the following exclamatory sentences and identify them.
1. That's so creative!
2. He saw a rooster, a tractor, and a giant football, all made of cement!
3. Hurrah! India has won the match.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotions and ends with an exclamation mark (!). Can you name any four emotions?
D. Find at least five exclamatory sentences from your Grade 4 English textbook and identify the emotions or feelings expressed in them. Record them in the table: Sentence | Feeling/Emotion.

question_bank.stem: Find and record five exclamatory sentences from your Grade 4 English textbook with their emotions.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
E. Look at the pictures of the animal families and read the words given below each of them.
lion – lioness – lion cub
tiger – tigress – tiger cub
rooster – hen – chick
bull – cow – calf
stallion – mare – foal

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Illustrations of five animal family groups with gender/age labels

tasks_verbatim.task_text: F. Look at the picture below. Would you like to add anything to this picture? Use your crayons and colour pencils to add to the picture.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Illustration of a rural Punjab scene (context for water tank chapter) for colouring and creative addition

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Design a poster in the space below, encouraging people to preserve and clean old water tanks. Include:
• Topic: 'Save Our Water Tanks — A Community Awareness Campaign'
• A catchy slogan (e.g., 'Save Tanks, Save Lives!').
• A few bullet points on why tanks are important.
• An invitation to join a cleaning drive or awareness programme (mention the place and time of the cleaning drive).

B. Write 10 words that end with 'er'. For example: water.
Now, do the same with: 'or' and 'at'. Write the words in your notebook.

A. You have read a lot about tanks. Have you ever wondered how these tanks are cleaned? Find out about the techniques for cleaning water tanks. You may bring relevant pictures or photographs. Prepare a chart and put it up in your class.

B. Look at the pictures given below. The story may already be familiar to you — The Thirsty Crow. The crow has become famous for its innovative idea. Imagine that your school has invited the crow to interact with the students. Enact the event with the help of your teacher.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Sequential pictures illustrating The Thirsty Crow story
